text: The surgeon uses three left-sided ports to access the surgical site. The small bowel is easily mobilized, and the omentum is being removed. Ethobond sutures from the previous repair are visible near the hernia defect.

The surgeon examines the surgical field after clearing adhesions, identifying the superior hernia defect above the peritoneal fold and falciform ligament. The retrorectus dissection begins at the inferior edge of the progrip mesh from the previous operation. Attempts to remove the mesh reveal it is adhered to the muscle, prompting the surgeon to detach the posterior sheath from the mesh instead, and proceed with dissection inferiorly.

The surgeon dissects at the level of the umbilicus, addressing a small umbilical defect and proceeding along the medial edge of the right rectus muscle. Contributory perforator vessels from the epigastrics are visible, and care is taken to preserve the epigastrics while dissecting laterally to separate the posterior rectus sheath from the rectus muscle. The surgeon returns to the challenging area, attempting to detach the posterior sheath from the pro-grip mesh, marking a line towards the superior lateral edge of the defect to extend the dissection laterally beyond the mesh.

The surgeon is dissecting the posterior sheath from the ring of the hernia in the upper abdomen, with mesh visible above. A neurovascular bundle is identified during the dissection. The cut transversus muscle is noted, and the surgeon attempts to take down the peritoneum for better visualization, aiming to preserve the peritoneum as a posterior layer.

The surgeon examines the peritoneum above the defect, struggling to identify the lateral edge. They open the peritoneum to improve visibility and realize the posterior sheath has been opened further laterally. The surgeon then moves to the right lower quadrant below the arcuate line, stripping down the superficial transversalis to access the TAR plane. They create small holes in the fascia, which are closed later, and continue lateral dissection while maintaining a bloodless field.

The surgeon works laterally to transition the dissection medially, focusing on taking down the transverse allofascia to expose the bare muscle of the transversus abdominis (TA). The dissection reveals neurovascular bundles, which the surgeon carefully avoids by staying medial. The surgeon identifies that the previous maneuver inadvertently hooked the transversus abdominis, leading to an upper transversus abdominis release (TAR), which is now being completed.

The surgeon re-enters the pre-transversalis plane, carefully dissecting to remove the peritoneum and attenuated hernia sac, thereby exposing the edge of the hernia defect. The dissection progresses smoothly in the unviolated space, facilitating the continuation of the transversus abdominis release.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection near the costal margin, navigating the retro-rectus space and ensuring posterior coverage. The dissection progresses smoothly, revealing the lateral edge of the hernia defect and maintaining a clear view of anatomical structures.

The surgeon approaches the xiphoid process, revealing diaphragmatic fibers. The cut edge of the transversus abdominis muscle is visible, along with the superior and inferior hernia defects. The surgeon prepares to begin the left-sided dissection by placing right-sided ports, mirroring the initial setup.
